content:
# Exodus Chapter 3 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~32v–33r (PDF pp. 63–64); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 43–44 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_003_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_003_page_002.jpg`

---

**3:1** Now Moses was shepherding the flock of Jethro his father-in-law, the priest of Midian. And he led the flock beyond the wilderness and came to Horeb, the mountain of God.

**3:2** And the angel of YHWH appeared to him in a flame of fire from the midst of the thornbush. And he looked, and behold, the thornbush was burning with fire, yet the thornbush was not consumed.

**3:3** And Moses said, "Let me turn aside now and see this great sight -- why the thornbush does not burn up."

**3:4** And YHWH saw that he turned aside to see, and God called to him from the midst of the thornbush and said, "Moses! Moses!" And he said, "Here I am."

**3:5** And He said, "Do not draw near to this place. Remove your sandals from your feet, for the place upon which you are standing is holy ground."

**3:6** And He said, "I am the God of your father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob." And Moses hid his face, for he was afraid to look upon God.

**3:7** And YHWH said, "I have surely seen the affliction of My people who are in Egypt, and I have heard their cry because of their taskmasters, for I know their sufferings.

**3:8** And I have come down to deliver them from the hand of Egypt, and to bring them up from that land to a good and spacious land, to a land flowing with milk and honey -- to the place of the Canaanite and the Hittite and the Amorite and the Perizzite and the Hivite and the Jebusite.

**3:9** And now, behold, the cry of the sons of Israel has come to Me, and I have also seen the oppression with which the Egyptians oppress them.

**3:10** And now, go, and I will send you to Pharaoh, that you may bring My people, the sons of Israel, out of Egypt."

**3:11** And Moses said to God, "Who am I, that I should go to Pharaoh, and that I should bring the sons of Israel out of Egypt?"

**3:12** And He said, "For I will be with you, and this shall be the sign for you that I have sent you: when you have brought the people out of Egypt, you shall worship God upon this mountain."

**3:13** And Moses said to God, "Behold, when I come to the sons of Israel and say to them, 'The God of your fathers has sent me to you,' and they say to me, 'What is His name?' -- what shall I say to them?"

**3:14** And God said to Moses, "Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh." And He said, "Thus you shall say to the sons of Israel: 'Ehyeh has sent me to you.'" [fn]

**3:15** And God said further to Moses, "Thus you shall say to the sons of Israel: 'YHWH, the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, has sent me to you.' This is My name forever, and this is My memorial from generation to generation.

**3:16** Go, and gather the elders of Israel, and say to them, 'YHWH, the God of your fathers, has appeared to me, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, saying: I have surely attended to you and to what is being done to you in Egypt.

**3:17** And I have said, I will bring you up from the affliction of Egypt to the land of the Canaanite and the Hittite and the Amorite and the Perizzite and the Hivite and the Jebusite, to a land flowing with milk and honey.'

**3:18** And they will listen to your voice. And you shall come, you and the elders of Israel, to the king of Egypt, and you shall say to him, 'YHWH, the God of the Hebrews, has met with us. And now, let us go, we pray, a journey of three days into the wilderness, that we may sacrifice to YHWH our God.'

**3:19** But I know that the king of Egypt will not let you go, except by a mighty hand.

**3:20** And I will stretch out My hand and strike Egypt with all My wonders which I will do in its midst, and after that he will send you out.

**3:21** And I will give this people favor in the eyes of the Egyptians, and it shall be that when you go, you shall not go empty-handed.

**3:22** And each woman shall ask of her neighbor and of the woman sojourning in her house, articles of silver and articles of gold, and garments. And you shall put them upon your sons and upon your daughters, and you shall plunder Egypt."

---

> **[fn] v.14** — Hebrew: *Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh* (אֶהְיֶ֖ה אֲשֶׁ֣ר אֶהְיֶ֑ה). The verb *ehyeh* is the first-person singular imperfect of *hayah* ("to be"), encompassing present and future: "I Am" and "I Will Be." The full phrase holds open a field of meaning that no single English rendering can close: "I Am Who I Am," "I Will Be What I Will Be," "I Am the One Who Causes Being." The SOPHIA Bible preserves the Hebrew in the text and provides this note so the reader encounters the Name as Moses did -- irreducible, self-interpreting, and open. See Council Notes below.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### 1. The Divine Name: *Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh* (v.14) -- ⚠️ FLAGGED

This is the supreme crux of the chapter and one of the most consequential translation decisions in all of Scripture.

**The three translators diverged:**
- **EMET** transliterated: "Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh [I-Am Who I-Am / I-Will-Be Who I-Will-Be]" and retained "Ehyeh" as the name sent to Israel. EMET preserves maximum Hebrew fidelity but requires brackets to communicate meaning.
- **LOGOS** rendered: "I AM WHO I AM" and "I AM has sent me to you." This follows the traditional English rendering (KJV/ESV/NASB tradition), capitalizing to mark divine-name status.
- **PNEUMA** also rendered: "I AM WHO I AM" and "I AM has sent me to you," matching LOGOS, but noted in commentary that the Hebrew resists reduction to any single English tense.

**SOPHIA's decision:** The consensus retains the Hebrew *Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh* in the translation text, with a footnote providing the full semantic range. The reasons:

1. **The Hebrew is a name, not a sentence.** God is not explaining a philosophical concept; He is disclosing a Name. Names are transliterated, not translated. We transliterate Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, YHWH -- and *Ehyeh* is the first-person form of that same Name.
2. **No English rendering is adequate.** "I AM WHO I AM" collapses the imperfect tense into a static present. "I WILL BE WHAT I WILL BE" loses the present-tense force. The Hebrew holds both simultaneously. Translating forces a choice the original does not make.
3. **The first-to-third-person shift is preserved.** In v.14, God says *Ehyeh* ("I am/will be") -- first person. In v.15, the same reality is communicated as *YHWH* ("He is/will be") -- third person. Transliterating *Ehyeh* in v.14 and *YHWH* in v.15 preserves this grammatical-theological architecture: what God calls Himself differs in person from what Israel calls Him.
4. **EMET's approach is vindicated by the other two translators' own notes.** Both LOGOS and PNEUMA, in their commentary, acknowledged the inadequacy of "I AM WHO I AM." The footnote system allows us to honor what all three translators recognized.

This decision aligns with EMET against LOGOS and PNEUMA on the translation text, but incorporates the interpretive clarity that LOGOS and PNEUMA provided through the footnote apparatus.

### 2. The Divine Name YHWH (throughout: vv. 2, 4, 7, 15, 16, 18)

**The three translators diverged:**
- **EMET** transliterated: YHWH throughout.
- **LOGOS** rendered: "the LORD" throughout, following the Masoretic *qere perpetuum* (Adonai) and English convention.
- **PNEUMA** transliterated: YHWH throughout.

**SOPHIA's decision:** YHWH is retained as a transliteration. Two of three translators agreed (EMET and PNEUMA). The Tetragrammaton is a proper noun -- God's personal name -- and proper nouns are transliterated, not translated. The Masoretic reading tradition of substituting *Adonai* is a liturgical practice, not a translation principle. The SOPHIA Bible is a study translation that privileges textual fidelity. Readers may follow whatever pronunciation tradition they hold (Adonai, HaShem, Yahweh, or reverential silence), but the text presents what the Leningrad Codex writes: the four letters yod-he-vav-he.

### 3. "Thornbush" vs. "Bush" (vv. 2-4)

**The three translators diverged:**
- **EMET** transliterated *seneh* with "thornbush" in brackets.
- **LOGOS** rendered: "bush."
- **PNEUMA** rendered: "thornbush."

**SOPHIA's decision:** "Thornbush" is adopted. The Hebrew *seneh* is a specific desert thorn species, not a generic bush. Two of three translators favored the specific rendering, and the specificity matters theologically: God appears in the lowliest of plants. The possible wordplay with *Sinai* is noted.

### 4. "Holy ground" vs. "Ground of holiness" (v.5)

**The three translators diverged:**
- **EMET** preserved the construct chain: "ground of holiness."
- **LOGOS** rendered naturally: "holy ground."
- **PNEUMA** compressed to: "The ground where you are standing is holy."

**SOPHIA's decision:** "Holy ground" is adopted. While EMET's literal rendering preserves the Hebrew construct (*admat qodesh*), the phrase "holy ground" has become so deeply embedded in English religious consciousness -- and so faithfully conveys the meaning -- that departing from it would create unnecessary strangeness without proportional gain. This is one case where the formal rendering achieves what the literal and dynamic renderings also aim for.

### 5. "I have surely seen" -- Infinitive Absolute (v.7)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "Seeing I have seen" -- wooden but preserves the doubling.
- **LOGOS:** "I have surely seen" -- natural English intensification.
- **PNEUMA:** "I have seen -- truly seen" -- dynamic emphasis with dash.

**SOPHIA's decision:** "I have surely seen" is adopted (following LOGOS). This is the most readable English intensification that preserves the emphatic force of *ra'oh ra'iti* without the awkwardness of "seeing I have seen." The same principle applies to *paqod paqadti* in v.16 ("I have surely attended to you").

### 6. "The angel of YHWH" to "YHWH" / "God" transition (vv. 2-4)

All three translators preserved this transition without harmonization: *mal'akh YHWH* in v.2, then YHWH and God speaking directly from v.4 onward. The manuscript confirms this reading clearly. The SOPHIA consensus preserves this theologically significant fluidity -- the messenger and the sender are distinguished yet unified -- without editorial smoothing.

### 7. "Your father" singular (v.6)

All three translators correctly rendered *avikha* as singular ("your father"), distinguishing it from the plural "your fathers" in vv.13, 15, 16. The Leningrad Codex is clear on this reading. The SOPHIA consensus preserves the distinction.

### 8. "Except by a mighty hand" (v.19)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "and not by a strong hand" -- preserving the MT ambiguity.
- **LOGOS:** "except by a mighty hand" -- resolving with the LXX.
- **PNEUMA:** "unless a mighty hand forces him" -- resolving dynamically.

**SOPHIA's decision:** "except by a mighty hand" is adopted. The narrative context (vv. 20-22 describe the compulsion) and the LXX (*ean me meta cheiros krataias*) both support the exceptive reading. EMET's preservation of ambiguity is admirable, but in this case the narrative flow makes the meaning clear, and the consensus reading follows LOGOS.

### 9. "Worship" vs. "Serve" vs. "Sacrifice" (v.12)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "you-shall-serve God upon this mountain" (from *ta'avdun*).
- **LOGOS:** "you shall serve God upon this mountain."
- **PNEUMA:** "you will worship God on this very mountain."

**SOPHIA's decision:** "You shall worship God upon this mountain" is adopted. The Hebrew *ta'avdun* (from *'avad*) can mean "serve" or "worship." In the context of a theophanic encounter at the sacred mountain, "worship" captures the specific cultic sense. PNEUMA's insight is correct here, while the verb's broader sense of service is preserved in other contexts.

### 10. "Plunder" (v.22)

All three translators agreed on the force of *venitsaltem* (Piel of *natsal*): plunder, despoil, strip. EMET used "despoil," LOGOS used "plunder," PNEUMA used "strip Egypt bare." The consensus adopts "plunder" -- strong, clear, and carrying the connotation of just seizure. The stripping of Egypt's wealth is restitution for generations of unpaid forced labor.

### 11. "Israelites" vs. "Sons of Israel" (throughout)

- **EMET** and **LOGOS:** "sons of Israel" (*bney Yisra'el*).
- **PNEUMA:** alternated between "Israelites" and "sons of Israel."

**SOPHIA's decision:** "Sons of Israel" is retained throughout. The Hebrew *bney Yisra'el* is literally "sons of Israel" and carries the familial, covenantal connotation of descent from the patriarch Jacob/Israel. "Israelites" is an acceptable English gloss, but "sons of Israel" preserves the genealogical bond that is theologically operative in this passage -- God is addressing the descendants of the man whose name He changed.

---

## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### ⚠️ v.14 — *Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh*: The Divine Self-Naming
| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh [I-Am Who I-Am / I-Will-Be Who I-Will-Be] | Transliteration + dual bracketed gloss |
| LOGOS | I AM WHO I AM | Traditional English rendering (present tense) |
| PNEUMA | I AM WHO I AM | Dynamic rendering matching traditional (with extended note) |

**Nature of disagreement:** Whether to transliterate or translate the divine self-naming. EMET transliterates; LOGOS and PNEUMA translate identically but both acknowledge in their notes that the English flattens the Hebrew.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Transliteration (*Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh*) with footnote providing the full semantic range. See Council Note 1 above for full reasoning.

**Severity:** HIGH. This is the foundational theophanic moment of the Hebrew Bible. The choice between transliteration and translation shapes how every subsequent reader encounters the God of Israel. No resolution is without loss.

### ⚠️ v.15 — YHWH: The Tetragrammaton
| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | YHWH | Transliteration of the consonantal Tetragrammaton |
| LOGOS | the LORD | Traditional English substitute following *qere* |
| PNEUMA | YHWH | Transliteration of the consonantal Tetragrammaton |

**Nature of disagreement:** Whether to transliterate the divine name or follow the ancient substitution practice.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** YHWH (transliteration), following the 2-to-1 majority and the principle that proper nouns are transliterated. See Council Note 2 above.

**Severity:** MODERATE. This is a long-standing translation tradition debate. The SOPHIA Bible's choice to transliterate is a principled departure from centuries of English convention ("the LORD"), but it aligns with the growing scholarly and ecumenical consensus that the Name should be visible in, though not necessarily pronounced from, the text.

### ⚠️ v.5 — "Holy ground" vs. "Ground of holiness"
| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | ground of holiness | Literal construct chain |
| LOGOS | holy ground | Natural English equivalent |
| PNEUMA | The ground where you are standing is holy | Dynamic paraphrase |

**Nature of disagreement:** How to render the Hebrew construct *admat qodesh*.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Holy ground" (following LOGOS). The construct chain is faithfully rendered by the English adjective-noun construction, and the phrase carries deep resonance in English religious usage. EMET's "ground of holiness" is more literal but not more accurate.

**Severity:** LOW. All three renderings convey the same theological reality. The difference is stylistic.

---

*Consilience achieved across 22 verses. Three verses flagged with disagreements (vv. 5, 14, 15). The supreme crux -- God's self-naming in v.14 -- remains irreducible; the SOPHIA consensus preserves the Hebrew and trusts the reader with a footnote rather than foreclosing the Name's meaning.*

-- SOPHIA
